event: God the Father Enthroned with Saints
symbols: throne — divine sovereignty and the seat of ultimate authority over creation
lunette with cherubim — the heavenly realm surrounding God's presence
architectural frame with pediment — the temple or house of God, a threshold between heaven and earth
halo around the central figure — divine glory and uncreated light
flanking saints — the communion of saints who stand in the presence of God
columns — pillars of the Church and the stability of divine order
lamps or candelabra — liturgical light symbolizing the presence of the Holy Spirit
description: A fresco within an architectural niche shaped as a pedimented aedicule. In the upper lunette, a blessing figure surrounded by winged cherub heads sits in a mandorla against a dark ground. Below, in the main register, an elderly bearded figure sits enthroned at center, wearing robes and holding a staff or crozier. He is flanked by two standing saints: a figure on the left in flowing robes holding a book, and a figure on the right in armor or richly decorated garments. The composition is framed by painted columns and pilasters, with small hanging lamps on either side of the throne.
theological_description: This fresco presents a traditional depiction of God the Father enthroned in majesty, a subject rooted in the prophetic visions of Isaiah 6 and Ezekiel 1, where the Lord sits upon a high and exalted throne surrounded by angelic beings. The cherubim in the lunette recall the seraphim of Isaiah's temple vision who cry 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of hosts.' The architectural frame evokes the heavenly temple described in Revelation 4, where the One seated on the throne is worshipped by the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders. The flanking saints standing in attendance represent the communion of saints who, having been justified by grace, now stand before the throne of God. The entire composition functions as a visual articulation of Psalm 11:4: 'The Lord is in his holy temple; the Lord's throne is in heaven.' The centrality and elevation of the divine figure affirm the orthodox doctrine of God's transcendence and absolute sovereignty over all creation, while the accessibility of the flanking saints affirms that through Christ, believers have access to the Father (Ephesians 2:18).

scripture_refs.reason: The enthroned Lord surrounded by angelic beings directly parallels Isaiah's vision of God high and lifted up in the temple with seraphim around him
The throne of God surrounded by heavenly beings and worshippers reflects the Revelation 4 vision of the heavenly throne room
The Lord enthroned in his holy temple with his throne in heaven corresponds to the centrality and elevation of the divine figure
Ezekiel's vision of a figure with the appearance of a man seated on a sapphire throne above the cherubim prefigures this depiction
The saints standing in the presence of God reflect the Pauline teaching that through Christ we have access to the Father
The Lord reigns, he is enthroned upon the cherubim — directly matching the iconography of deity above cherub heads
